text: The surgeon begins a laparoscopic hysterectomy using the da Vinci Surgical System, with two instruments inserted into the patient's abdomen. The left hand holds a grasper equipped with bipolar electrical energy for cautery.

The surgeon begins a laparoscopic hysterectomy using the da Vinci Surgical System. Two robotic instruments are visible in the patient's abdomen: a grasper with bipolar electrical energy in the left hand for cautery, and fine monopolar scissors in the right hand, also capable of cauterizing tissue. The abdomen is insufflated with carbon dioxide gas, creating a working space between the organs and instruments.
The surgeon holds small, fine monopolar scissors in the right hand, which are used to cut and cauterize tissue during the laparoscopic hysterectomy.

The surgeon has insufflated the abdomen with carbon dioxide gas, creating a working space between the organs and instruments. The retroperitoneum is being opened, and the ovarian vessels are being skeletonized, revealing their structure for further dissection.

The surgeon opens the retroperitoneum and skeletonizes the ovarian vessels, visible as a pedicle supplying blood to the ovary and fallopian tube. Using a bipolar instrument, the surgeon cauterizes and divides these vessels, ensuring minimal bleeding.
The surgeon uses a bipolar instrument to cauterize and divide the ovarian vessels, which are visible as a pedicle providing blood supply to the ovary and fallopian tube.

The surgeon uses instruments to gain control over the ovarian blood vessels, minimizing bleeding. A third instrument, operated by the assistant surgeon, holds the uterus in place at an optimal angle for the procedure.

The assistant surgeon holds the uterus in place, maintaining an optimal angle for the procedure. The primary surgeon uses monopolar scissors to cut across the ovarian vessels, demonstrating efficient and precise instrument movement without hand shake.

The surgeon uses monopolar scissors to cut across the ovarian vessels, demonstrating precise and efficient movement of the instruments without any hand shake, facilitated by the da Vinci Surgical System.

The surgeon uses robotic instruments to dissect the bladder flap, carefully separating the bladder from the cervix and upper part of the vagina to keep it out of the surgical field during the hysterectomy.
The surgeon dissects the bladder flap, carefully separating the bladder from the cervix and upper part of the vagina to keep it out of the surgical field during the hysterectomy.
The surgeon begins skeletonizing the uterine vessels, which are visible on the sides and towards the center of the surgical field, preparing them for coagulation and division.

The surgeon begins skeletonizing the uterine vessels, which are visible as they extend from the sides towards the center of the uterus. This process involves carefully exposing the vessels in preparation for coagulation and division.

The surgical team moves to the opposite side of the surgical field. The assistant uses a conventional laparoscopic instrument, which lacks a wristed component and moves with three degrees of freedom, contrasting with the robotic instruments that offer six degrees of freedom.

The surgeon uses robotic instruments to divide the round ligament on the opposite side, controlling bleeding from a small blood vessel within the ligament. The assistant uses a conventional laparoscopic instrument with limited movement, while the robotic instruments provide enhanced dexterity and a magnified view of the surgical field.

The surgeon divides the round ligament using precise instruments, controlling bleeding from a small blood vessel within the ligament. The magnified camera view provides a close-up of the tissue, enhancing visibility of the surgical field.

The surgeon evacuates smoke through a special vent to maintain a clear view. On the patient's left side, the space is opened to begin skeletonizing the ovarian vessels, with careful attention to the nearby colon.

The surgeon evacuates smoke through a special vent to maintain a clear view. On the patient's left side, the surgeon opens up space to skeletonize the ovarian vessels, carefully navigating around the colon. A small window is created to pass instruments through, facilitating the skeletonization of the ovarian vessels.

The surgeon creates a small window in the tissue to facilitate the passage of instruments, aiming to skeletonize the ovarian vessels. The binocular camera provides a three-dimensional view of the surgical field.

The surgeon has dissected both ovaries and tubes free and begins working on the bladder flap on the left side, ensuring careful dissection to avoid bladder injury during the hysterectomy. The ureters, which run close to the cervix, are also dissected away to minimize the risk of injury when cauterizing the blood supply.

Both ovaries and fallopian tubes are visibly dissected free. The surgeon begins working on the bladder flap on the patient's left side, carefully dissecting it to avoid injury to the bladder during the hysterectomy.
The surgeon carefully dissects the ureters away from the cervix in the female pelvis, ensuring they are safely separated before cauterizing the blood supply to minimize the risk of injury.

The surgeon carefully dissects the bladder away from the uterus, ensuring it is safely moved to prevent injury during the hysterectomy. The assistant repositions instruments due to limited movement capabilities. Behind the uterus, the rectum is visible with a layer of fat, and efforts are made to keep it protected during the procedure.

The surgeon carefully dissects the bladder away from the uterus, with the assistant occasionally re-grasping due to limited movement. Behind the uterus, the rectum is visible with a layer of fat, and efforts are made to keep it safe from surgical harm.

The surgeon skeletonizes the uterine vessels on the patient's left side, coagulates them, and then divides them using robotic technology. This step is visually observable as part of the hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon skeletonizes the uterine vessels on the patient's left side, coagulates them, and then divides them using surgical instruments. This step is a standard part of the hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon ensures that the blood vessels are securely controlled and cauterized, emphasizing the precision required in this straightforward hysterectomy procedure.

The surgeon ensures that the blood vessels are securely controlled and cauterized using robotic instruments, emphasizing the precision of the technique. The robotic tools provide stability and enhanced control compared to conventional laparoscopic tools.

The surgeon divides the uterine vessels on the patient's right side using robotic instruments, effectively controlling the blood supply to the uterus. Following this, the focus shifts to dissecting the cervix free, nearing the completion of the hysterectomy.

The surgeon divides the uterine vessels on the patient's right side, effectively controlling the blood supply to the uterus. A member of the surgical team introduces a vaginal probe, visible as movement in the lower part of the surgical field.

A member of the surgical team inserts a vaginal probe into the vagina, which distends the bladder away from the cervix. The surgeon uses precise control to clean the bladder flap and begins to control the remaining blood supply to the cervix, aided by the distension provided by the probe.
The surgeon uses a vaginal probe to distend the vagina and separate the bladder from the cervix, facilitating safer cervical dissection while ensuring the bladder and ureters remain protected.
The surgeon has secured the uterine vessels and begins cleaning the bladder flap with precise control. The vaginal probe is visible, aiding in distending the surrounding tissues.
The surgeon uses precise instruments to control the remaining blood supply to the cervix, ensuring hemostasis during the hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon prepares to remove the specimen, followed by the suturing phase of the operation, utilizing the robotic system's enhanced dexterity for precise wrist movements during suturing.

The surgeon uses monopolar energy scissors to skeletonize the junction between the vagina and cervix, carefully defining and separating these tissues in preparation for specimen removal and subsequent suturing.
The surgeon uses monopolar energy scissors to skeletonize the junction between the vagina and cervix, carefully defining and separating the two tissues.
The surgeon uses scissors to separate the cervix from the top of the vagina, cutting across the vaginal tissue. A white plastic-coated probe is visible within the vagina, aiding in the procedure.

The surgeon uses scissors to separate the cervix from the top of the vagina, with a white plastic-coated probe visible in the vagina. A grasper is introduced through the vagina to retrieve the specimen, which is then removed through the vaginal opening. The fallopian tube is visible during this process, and the specimen is sent to the pathologist.

The cervix is now completely separated from the top of the vagina. A surgical team member inserts a grasper through the vagina, with the patient's legs slightly separated, to retrieve the specimen. The specimen is placed between the grasper's jaws and extracted through the vaginal opening.
The surgeon demonstrates the removal of the uterus through the vaginal canal, utilizing small incisions made during the hysterectomy procedure.
The fallopian tube is visible before being removed, and the specimen is extracted and prepared for pathology. The surgeon exchanges the right-handed instrument for the next step.

The surgeon exchanges the right-handed instrument for a needle driver and begins suturing to close the top of the vagina, known as the vaginal cuff closure. The self-locking suture device is used to ensure a secure and precise closure, with the suture designed to dissolve within eight to twelve weeks.

The specimen is removed, and a needle driver is introduced to the surgical field. The surgeon holds the needle driver in the right hand while the surgical tech provides a suture to close the top of the vagina.
The surgeon uses a self-locking suture device to close the vaginal cuff, demonstrating the secure and precise closure technique.
The surgeon uses a self-locking suture to close the vaginal cuff, threading the suture through a loop to create the first knot, which will dissolve in eight to twelve weeks, ensuring smooth healing.

The surgeon uses a needle driver to bring the suture through the loop, creating the first knot to secure the vaginal cuff. The suturing process continues with precise wrist movements, similar to opening a doorknob, allowing for intuitive control over the needle's path through the tissue.
The surgeon continues suturing the entire vaginal cuff using laparoscopic techniques, demonstrating the wrist movement required to maneuver the needle, similar to turning a doorknob.
The surgeon uses a needle driver to precisely drive the needle through the vaginal cuff tissue, demonstrating controlled suturing movements to close the cuff efficiently.

The surgeon completes the closure by bringing the uterosacral ligaments together to support the pelvic floor. The final stitch is placed, and the robotic system is undocked. The needle is retrieved from the patient's abdomen using scissors, followed by closing the incisions, marking the completion of the operation.

The surgeon completes the closure by suturing the uterosacral ligaments together, providing support and structure to the pelvic floor. The final stitch is placed, marking the completion of the operation.

The surgeon throws the final stitch using the robotic system, then undocks the robot. A scissor is used to make a quick cut to retrieve the needle from the patient's abdomen, followed by closing the incisions.
